title: Stapelia neliana
taxonomy: Stapelia neliana is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae, commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. It is a member of the genus Stapelia, which is a group of succulent plants native to southern Africa. The genus Stapelia is further classified under the subfamily Asclepiadoideae, which is a group of plants characterized by their unique flowers and ability to produce toxic compounds. Stapelia neliana is a relatively rare and lesser-known species within the Stapelia genus. This plant is known for its unique appearance and ability to produce a strong, unpleasant odor.
uses: Stapelia neliana has been studied for its potential medicinal properties, including its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. The plant's extracts may have applications in wound healing and skin care due to their ability to promote tissue repair and reduce inflammation. Additionally, Stapelia neliana has been investigated for its antiviral and antioxidant properties, which could be beneficial in the treatment of various diseases. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal uses and potential benefits. intro: Stapelia neliana, also known as the starfish cactus or night-blooming Stapelia, is a species of cactus in the family Apocynaceae, characterized by its unique, star-shaped flowers that bloom at night and emit a strong, unpleasant odor to attract pollinators. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, particularly in South Africa, to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems, due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Stapelia neliana include alkaloids, glycosides, and iridoid glucosides, which are believed to be responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Stapelia neliana is often used to make infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, as well as being incorporated into topical creams and ointments to treat skin conditions and wounds.
